coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs)-based afterglow materials have attracted extensive attention due to their low cost, adjustable optical properties, and low toxicity. However, It is urgent to develop afterglow CDs with both color-tunable solid-state fluorescence and room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP), especially deep red-emitting afterglow. Herein, oxygen-containing groups modified CDs (O-CDs) and nitrogen-containing groups modified CDs (N-CDs) with greenish-yellow fluorescence emission were synthesized by electrochemical method, respectively. After combining with boric acid (BA), the luminescence of O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites shifts to blue (460 nm) and deep red (652 nm and 693 nm) region by regulating the surface B-O and B-N bonds, respectively. The covalency can be regulated by the bond length of the B-O and B-N bonds of the CDs/BA composites, thereby regulating the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), which in turn dominates the luminescence energy gap and produces blue-shift and red-shift luminescence. The O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites also possess green (550 nm) and deep red (666 nm and 697 nm) RTP emission, which is mainly due to the triplet emission stabilized by the covalent bonds of B-O, B-N and B-C bonds formed by CDs and BA matrix. In addition, O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites can be applied to multi-level anti-counterfeiting and data encryption.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical synthesis of carbon dots with blue and deep red fluorescence and room temperature phosphorescence via surface state regulation of covalency

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs)-based afterglow materials have attracted extensive attention due to their low cost, adjustable optical properties, and low toxicity. However, It is urgent to develop afterglow CDs with both color-tunable solid-state fluorescence and room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP), especially deep red-emitting afterglow. Herein, oxygen-containing groups modified CDs (O-CDs) and nitrogen-containing groups modified CDs (N-CDs) with greenish-yellow fluorescence emission were synthesized by electrochemical method, respectively. After combining with boric acid (BA), the luminescence of O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites shifts to blue (460 nm) and deep red (652 nm and 693 nm) region by regulating the surface B-O and B-N bonds, respectively. The covalency can be regulated by the bond length of the B-O and B-N bonds of the CDs/BA composites, thereby regulating the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), which in turn dominates the luminescence energy gap and produces blue-shift and red-shift luminescence. The O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites also possess green (550 nm) and deep red (666 nm and 697 nm) RTP emission, which is mainly due to the triplet emission stabilized by the covalent bonds of B-O, B-N and B-C bonds formed by CDs and BA matrix. In addition, O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites can be applied to multi-level anti-counterfeiting and data encryption.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), serving as novel luminescent nanomaterials, possess distinctive advantages, including remarkable luminescence properties, low toxicity, and simple preparation techniques, which have aroused widespread interest across numerous research fields [1,2]. The afterglow performance of CDs has been rising as a new star in recent years [3,4]. The fluorescence and phosphorescence of CDs are derived from the spin-allowed singlet and spin-forbidden triplet exciton transitions, respectively, which leads to a huge difference in the exciton recombination luminescence time between the singlet state (at nanosecond scales) and the triplet state (at millisecond scales) [5]. The difference of exciton recombination time for CDs adds a time dimension to the previous luminescence color dimension, which broadens the multi-dimensional luminescence characteristics of CDs [6,7]. Therefore, CDs with tunable fluorescence and afterglow properties have potential applications in the field of anti-counterfeiting and information security [8]. The matrix recombination strategy can effectively improve the solid-state quenching of CDs and stabilize the triplet states of CDs to achieve afterglow emission [9–11]. However, it is difficult to regulate the emission wavelength due to the uncontrollable structure of CDs and the complexity of the interaction between CDs and matrix. Moreover, it is full of challenges to clarify the interaction mechanism between CDs and matrix on the luminescence since the complexation of carbon core and surface states for CDs.

The afterglow emission of CDs can be regulated by the carbonization degree, heteroatom doping and energy transfer strategies. In various matrix, boric acid (BA) is considered as an ideal host for afterglow generation of CDs, which can effectively inhibit the non-radiative transition of triplet excitons caused by the vibration of guest molecules [12]. With the increased crystallinity of CDs by changing the type of carbon sources, the CDs/BA composites achieved room temperature afterglow characteristics from cyan to red [13]. In addition, the solid-state fluorescence of CDs/BA composites was gradually red-shifted from 523 nm to 555 nm with the increase of CDs size and graphitization degree by the encapsulation and carbonization of CDs [14]. The green, yellow-green and orange phosphorescent emission of CDs/BA composites were realized via increasing the sulfur doping contents and oxidation degrees of CDs [15]. Besides, the CDs/BA composites and 2-naphthalenesulfonic-acid-substituted perylene diimide were used as energy donors and energy acceptors to obtain tunable afterglow colors (blue, blue-green, and chartreuse) and lifetimes through fluorescence resonance energy transfer [16]. The main role of BA matrix in the luminescent properties of CDs composites is to disperse and fix CDs [17–19]. The high carbonization degree of CDs and the introduction of defect states by doping usually lead to strong non-radiative transitions, which makes it difficult to obtain long wavelength emission, and the realization of red luminescence through energy transfer will result in energy loss. Therefore, the solid-state fluorescence and phosphorescence of CDs are mostly concentrated in the blue-green region. It is a great challenge to achieve long wavelength regulation after combining CDs with the matrix, especially to realize deep red solid-state luminescence.

Surface states have great effects on regulating the afterglow luminescence properties of CDs composites. The afterglow of CDs/BA composites was regulated from blue to yellow via increasing surface amination contents [20]. The emission peaks of CDs composites obtained by pyromellitic acid, trimesic acid, and terephthalic acid as precursors were 440, 407 and 430 nm, respectively. The phosphorescence color of CDs composites was adjusted between blue and purple by different surface structures via adjusting the distribution of carboxyl functional groups on the precursor structure [21]. The phosphorescence colors (blue, green, yellow, and brown) of the CDs composites could be effectively adjusted by increasing the degree of carbonization and surface oxidation [12]. The red shift of solid-state luminescence of multicolor CDs composites was mainly due to the increase of the ratio of graphite N to total N content and average particle size [22]. The CDs are mostly prepared by hydrothermal method, microwave method and other bottom-up synthesis methods using small molecule polymerization. In the bottom-up synthesis process, the carbon cores and surface groups of CDs will be simultaneously affected. Therefore, the surface regulation of CDs cannot avoid the influence of carbon cores, which leads to the unclear surface regulation mechanism of CDs. It is urgent and significant to further elucidate the regulation mechanism of surface state on the luminescence properties of CDs composites.

The rigid structure formed by hydrogen bonding and ionic interactions between the matrix and CDs protect triplet excitons from deactivation and suppress their non-radiative transitions, thereby achieving afterglow emission [4,24]. However, the weak strength of the hydrogen bond leads to low stability of the formed rigid structure, resulting in limited afterglow performance of CDs. High strength of ionic bond can form a robust and rigid structure that enhances the afterglow performance of CDs. However, the presence of ions results in a quenching effect on the luminescence performance of CDs, thus hindering the enhancement of their afterglow performance [9,10]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a robust framework that maintains structural integrity without compromising the luminescent performance of CDs.

During the hydrothermal polymerization of small molecules into CDs, carbon core and surface states are concurrently generated and modified, thereby significantly complicating the investigation of the surface state mechanisms [25,26]. The carbon core of electrochemically synthesized CDs exhibits a pristine structure with minimal surface functional groups. Importantly, the introduction of surface groups via electrochemical functionalization does not alter the intrinsic structure of the carbon core, allowing for precise modulation of the surface state of CDs as required [27,28]. This characteristic makes it particularly suitable for investigating the mechanisms of surface state interactions. Furthermore, the electrochemical method possesses several advantages, including a straightforward process, high controllability, and scalability for large-scale synthesis [29,30]. The CDs synthesized by electrochemical method can serve as good models for surface state regulation due to the clean carbon cores and easy surface modification.

In this work, BA was selected as the matrix, and the greenish-yellow fluorescent CDs were embedded in the BA to achieve tunable emission of solid-state fluorescence and RTP. The greenish-yellow fluorescent O-CDs (oxygen containing groups modified CDs) and N-CDs (nitrogen containing groups modified CDs) were synthesized by an electrochemical method, and then they were heat-treated with BA to obtain O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites. Compared with the greenish-yellow fluorescence emission of O-CDs and N-CDs, the solid-state fluorescence emission of O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites is located in the blue (460 nm) and deep red (652 and 693 nm) region, respectively. The O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites also possess green (550 nm) and deep red (666 and 697 nm) RTP emissions with lifetimes of 628 ms and 5 ms, respectively. The experiment and calculation results suggest that the blue shift and red shift of the solid-state luminescence of O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites are induced by the formation of B-O and B-N bonds, respectively. The formation of B-O and B-N bonds leads to the increase and decrease of the band gap, respectively. The combination of O-CDs and N-CDs with BA can form covalent bonds of B-O, B-N and B-C, which stabilizes the triplet emission, inhibits the non-composite coincidence, and contributes to RTP emission. Based on the dual emission of solid-state fluorescence and RTP, the O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites are successfully applied to anti-counterfeiting patterns and data encryption. The covalent bonds (B-C, B-O, and B-N) formed between green CDs and BA create a more rigid structure. This rigidity effectively suppresses the non-radiative transition of triplet excitons in CDs, stabilizes triplet emission and enhances the persistence luminescence performance. This work creatively realizes the dual-performance regulation of solid-state fluorescence and RTP of CDs, broadens the solid-state luminescence of CDs to the deep red wavelength region, and provides new choice materials for anti-counterfeiting and information security.

O-phthalic acid was acquired from Bide Pharmatech Ltd. Boric acid was bought from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. O-phenylenediamine was bought from Tianjin Jinke Fine Chemical Research Institute. Graphite rods (Φ 5 mm) were acquired from Tianjin Zhongnuo Carbon Co., Ltd. All reagents and chemicals are commercially available, and the purity is of the analytical grade, which is used directly without further purification. In this work, deionized water (18 MΩ·cm−1) was used in all experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites
ce:section.ce:para: The surface functionalized greenish-yellow fluorescent oxygen-containing CDs (O-CDs) and nitrogen-containing CDs (N-CDs) were prepared by an electrochemical method. Specifically, o-phthalic acid and o-phenylenediamine were dissolved as surface modifiers at room temperature to form a uniform aqueous solution. Subsequently, two graphite rods were placed in the above solution and subjected to a voltage of 60 V for 3 h. In detail, two parallel graphite rods with an electrode spacing of 1 cm were used as the cathode and anode in the electrochemical reaction. The aforementioned aqueous solution served as the electrolyte solution in the electrochemical process. Upon activation of the reaction voltage, the initial current density was 0.046 mA/mm2. The CDs solution was purified through three cycles of centrifugation (10000 rpm, 5 min) to eliminate large particulate impurities and subjected to three rounds of dialysis to remove small molecular weight impurities. Finally, the obtained solutions were further used as precursor solutions for O-CDs and N-CDs, respectively.

O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites were prepared based on the modified method [31]. Generally, boric acid (2.0 g) is dispersed into 40 mL of O-CDs or N-CDs solution to form a uniform solution. Afterwards, the homogeneous solution was heated in a dry oven at 155 °C for 6 h. Finally, the block products were crushed into powder to obtain O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure of O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites
ce:section.ce:para:
Liquid greenish-yellow fluorescent O-CDs and N-CDs were prepared by electrochemical method using o-phthalic acid and o-phenylenediamine as surface modifiers, respectively (Fig. 1
a and Fig. S1a-b). Subsequently, O-CDs and N-CDs were combined with BA matrix by heating to form encapsulated CDs composites (O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites). Surprisingly, O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites emit blue and deep red fluorescence under ultraviolet (UV) light irradiation at 365 nm (Fig. S2a-d), respectively. After the UV lamp is turned off, the O-CDs/BA composites emit bright green afterglow, and the N-CDs/BA composites emit deep red afterglow (Fig. S2e-f). In addition, the luminesce-nce properties of O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites under different reaction conditions were investigated, as shown in Fig. S3a-c and Fig. S4a-c. The O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites with the optimal luminescent properties were obtained at 155 °C for 6 h with an addition of 2.0 g BA. However, individual O-CDs, N-CDs and BA do not show luminescence after the same heat treatment (Fig. S5a-d and Fig. S6a-d). It indicates that the combination of CDs and BA may have a significant effect on luminescence. As shown in Fig. 1b-e, the morphologies of CDs and CDs/BA composites were investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM). Fig. 1b-c shows that O-CDs and N-CDs are spherical or quasi-spherical particles with good dispersibility, and the average particle sizes are 2.17 ± 0.07 and 1.90 ± 0.38 nm, respectively. The HR-TEM images display that the lattice spacing of O-CDs and N-CDs is 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) planes of graphite [32]. The TEM images of O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA indicate that O-CDs and N-CDs are successfully embedded in the BA matrix and uniformly dispersed (Fig. 1d-e). The average particle sizes of O-CDs and N- CDs in O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites are 2.25 ± 0.42 nm and 1.92 ± 0.32 nm, and the lattice spacing is 0.21 nm, which is similar to that of O-CDs and N-CDs before compounding. It indicates that there is no significant change of CDs after embedding in BA matrix. In addition, energy-dispersive spectrometer (EDS) spectra of O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites show the presence of B elements, which also confirms the combination of CDs and BA (Fig. S7a-b) [33,34]. As shown in Fig. S8, the X-ray Diffraction (XRD) patterns of O-CDs and N-CDs exhibit a broad peak at about 2θ = 25°, which is corresponded to the characteristic peak of CDs [35]. After compounding, the diffraction peaks of O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites are consistent with the characteristic peaks of BA, which also proves that CDs are successfully encapsulated in the BA matrix [16].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites
ce:section.ce:para:
As shown in Fig. 2
a-i, the luminescence properties of O-CDs, N-CDs, O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites were further explored. Fig. 2a-c exhibit that the optimal fluorescence emission peaks of O-CDs and N-CDs with excitation independence are located at 570 nm and 562 nm, and their average fluorescence lifetimes are calculated to be 1.43 ns and 4.01 ns, respectively (Table S1). As shown in Fig. 2d-e, the fluorescence emission of O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites is blue-shifted and red-shifted relative to O-CDs and.

N-CDs, respectively. The fluorescence emission peak of O-CDs/BA composites is blue-shifted to 460 nm, which is 110 nm blue-shift compared with O-CDs. The fluorescence emission peaks of N-CDs/BA composites are located at 652 nm and 693 nm, respectively, and the maximum red shift is 131 nm relative to N-CDs. The average fluorescence lifetimes of O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites are 15.19 ns and 5.08 ns, respectively (Fig. 2f and Table S1). Compared with O-CDs and N-CDs, the average fluorescence lifetimes of O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites are increased, indicating that the non-radiative recombination is weakened during the transition of excitons from excited state to ground state.[36] As shown in Table S2, the absolute quantum yields (QY) of O-CDs, N-CDs, O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites are 0.72 %, 1.23 %, 4.51 % and 3.14 %, respectively. It indicates that QY of O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites is higher than that of O-CDs and N-CDs, which discloses that CDs encapsulated in BA matrix can hinder the fluorescence quenching.[37] In addition, as shown in Table S3, the non-radiative rate constants (KnR) of O-CDs/BA (6.29 × 107 s−1) and N-CDs/BA (1.91 × 108 s−1) composites are smaller than that of O-CDs (6.94 × 108 s−1) and N-CDs (2.46 × 108 s−1), which also proves that CDs embedded in BA matrix can reduce the non-radiative recombination and inhibit the fluorescence quenching of CDs in solid state [38]. The phosphorescence quantum yields of O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA are 1.45 % and 1.17 %, respectively [39]. Subsequently, the afterglow properties of O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites were explored, as illustrated in Fig. 2g-i and Fig. S9a-b. The afterglow emission of O-CDs/BA composites is located in the green (550 nm) region, and the afterglow emission of N-CDs/BA composites is located in the deep red (666 and 697 nm) region. The average afterglow lifetimes of O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites are 628 ms and 5 ms, respectively (Table S4). Furthermore, as shown in Fig. S10a-b, the intensity of afterglow emission peaks of O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites decreases with the increase of temperature, which suggests that the afterglow property belongs to room temperature phosphorescence [40]. In addition, the excitation spectra display that the optimal excitation peaks of O-CDs, N-CDs, O-CDs/BA, and N-CDs/BA composites are located at 380 nm, 370 nm, 330 nm and 620 nm (Fig. S11a), respectively. As shown in Fig. S11b, the UV–vis absorption spectra show that the absorption peaks below 300 nm of O-CDs, N-CDs, O-CDs/BA, and N-CDs/BA composites originate from the π–π* transition of C=C bonds. The absorption peaks or absorption bands above 300 nm of four CDs originate from n–π* transition of surface functional groups (amino, carboxyl, carbonyl and hydroxyl groups, etc) [20]. The O-CDs/BA, N-CDs/BA composites and BA have wide absorption bands above 300 nm, which indicates that the broadening of the absorption spectra is related to the BA matrix [41]. The excitation spectra and broad absorption bands of O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites display a large degree of overlap. It presents that the surface groups of O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites have a great influence on solid-state luminescence [42].

In order to further understand the solid-state luminescence properties, the surface states of O-CDs, N-CDs, O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites were characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra. As shown in the Fig. 3
a, O-CDs possess characteristic peaks of O-H (∼3242 cm−1), C-H (∼3016 cm−1), C=O (∼1674 cm−1), and C-O (∼1037 cm−1) bonds [21]. Compared with O-CDs, new peaks of B-O (∼1413 cm−1) and B-C bonds (∼983 cm−1) appear in O-CDs/BA composites, which suggests that O-CDs and BA are combined by B-C and B-O covalent bonds [43]. In addition, N-CDs contain a series of feature peaks of O-H/N-H (∼3242 cm−1), C-H (∼3016 cm−1), C=O (∼1674 cm−1), N-H (∼1328 cm−1), C-N (∼1099 cm−1) and C-O (∼1037 cm−1) bonds [44]. In addition to the similar functional groups of N-CDs, N-CDs/BA composites also contain new functional groups such as B-O (∼1413 cm−1), B-C (∼983 cm−1) and B-N (∼721 cm−1) bonds. It indicates that N-CDs and BA are covalently bonded by B-O, B-C and B-N bonds [45]. As shown in Fig. 3b and Table S5, O-CDs contain C (284.36 eV, 63.53 %) and O (531.17 eV, 36.47 %) elements, while O-CDs/BA composites contain B (192.67 eV, 33.81 %), C (284.81 eV, 25.94 %) and O (532.48 eV, 40.25 %) elements [17]. The N-CDs consist of C (284.12 eV, 75.42 %), O (531.49 eV, 6.32 %), and N (398.31 eV, 18.26 %) elements, and N-CDs/BA composites contain C (285.07 eV, 19.87 %), O (532.92 eV, 41.48 %), N (400.55 eV, 4.80 %) and B (193.18 eV, 33.85 %) elements [14]. It discloses that the surface groups of O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites are bonded with BA to form B-related groups. In order to further explore the changes of surface groups after the combination of CDs with BA, the high-resolution C 1 s, O 1 s, N 1 s, and B 1 s spectra of O-CDs, N-CDs, O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites are further investigated (Fig. 3c-f, Table S6-9). As displayed in Fig. 3c and Table S6, the C 1 s spectra show that the C element in the O-CDs exists in the form of C=C/C-C (284.33 eV, 60.20 %), C-O (285.52 eV, 12.35 %) and C=O (288.13 eV, 27.45 %) bonds, and the C element in the O-CDs/BA composites exists in the form of C-B (284.41 eV, 49.17 %), C=C/C-C (285.40 eV, 30.08 %), C-O (286.39 eV, 13.25 %) and C=O (287.72 eV, 7.50 %) bonds [18]. The C 1 s spectra of N-CDs have four peaks located at 283.85 eV, 284.55 eV, 285.40 eV and 288.22 eV, corresponding to C=C/C-C (13.85 %), C-N (25.60 %), C-O (43.54 %), and C=O (17.01 %) bonds. There are five peaks in N-CDs/BA composites centered at 283.98 eV, 284.79 eV, 285.72 eV, 286.63 eV, and 289.28 eV, which is correlated to C-B (21.33 %), C=C/C-C (22.93 %), C-N (19.94 %), C-O (17.70 %) and C=O (18.10 %) bonds [46]. As shown in Fig. 3d and Table S7, the O 1 s spectrum of O-CDs exhibits two binding energies at 531.76 eV and 535.89 eV which is corresponded to C=O (68.23 %) and C-O (31.77 %) bonds. And the O 1 s spectrum of O-CDs/BA composites displays three peaks at 531.86 eV, 532.76 eV and 533.72 eV that are correlated to C=O (32.18 %), C-O (23.45 %) and O-B (44.37 %) bonds [22]. The O 1 s spectrum of N-CDs can be fitted with two peaks centered at 532.11 eV and 533.87 eV, belonging to C=O (78.85 %) and C-O (21.15 %) bonds. The O 1 s spectrum of N-CDs/BA composites can be fitted well with three peaks centered at 532.15 eV, 533.22 eV and 534.46 eV, corresponding to C=O (28.58 %), C-O (13.91 %) and O-B (57.51 %) bonds [12]. As illustrated in the N 1 s spectra of Fig. 3e, N-CDs contain only one peak at 399.09 eV which is attributed to NH2 (100 %). The N-CDs/BA composites consist of two peaks at 399.93 eV and 401.28 eV that are attributed to N-B (60.90 %) and NH2 (39.10 %), respectively (Table S8) [13]. For Fig. 3f and Table S9, the B 1 s spectra of O-CDs/BA composites can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 192.38 eV and 193.26 eV, which are assigned to B-C (57.90 %) and B-O (42.10 %) bonds. The B 1 s spectrum of N-CDs/BA composites can be fitted with three peaks centered at 192.22 eV, 193.17 eV, and 194.22 eV, corresponding to B-N (34.33 %), B-C (38.68 %) and B-O (26.99 %) bonds [47].

As shown in high-resolution mass (HRMS) spectra, the molecular formulas of fragment-165, fragment-135, fragment-209, and fragment-179 are [C8H5O4]−, [C7H7N2O]−, [C8H6BO6]−, and [C7H8N2BO3]−, respectively (Fig. S12a) [48]. The detailed structural characterization of the four CDs was conducted using 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) (Fig. S12b). The signals in the range of 6–8 ppm are assignable to aromatic hydrogen nuclei, which provides evidence that the surface modification groups have been successfully grafted onto the carbon core [22].

Based on previous results, the surface modification groups can be successfully bonded to the carbon core during the preparation of CDs by electrochemical method [49]. The FTIR and XPS results prove that carboxyl and amino groups are successfully bonded to the surface of O-CDs and N-CDs as their luminescent centers to dominate the greenish-yellow fluorescence emission. The XPS results reveal that the O-CDs are bonded to BA in the form of B-O and B-C bonds in the O-CDs/BA composites, and the N-CDs are bonded to BA in the form of B-O, B-C and B-N bonds in the N-CDs/BA composites. Therefore, it is reasonable to speculate that the B-O bonds formed by the combination of surface carboxyl groups with BA are the origin for the blue-shift fluorescence of O-CDs/BA composites. And the B-N bonds formed by the combination of surface amino groups with BA are the origin for the red-shift fluorescence of N-CDs/BA composites.

In order to further verify the blue-shift fluorescence of CDs/BA composites is related to the B-O bonds, the fluorescence properties of combination of carbonyl modified CDs (CDs-1) and hydroxyl modified CDs (CDs-2) with BA were explored (Fig. S13a-d). Compared with the optimal fluorescence emission of CDs-1 (416 nm), the optimal fluorescence emission of CDs-1/BA composites (407 nm) is blue-shifted. Similarly, the fluorescence emission peak of CDs-2/BA composites (437 nm) also presents a blue shift relative to that of CDs-2 (477 nm and 540 nm). Subsequently, in order to confirm the red-shift fluorescence of CDs/BA composites is related to the B-N bonds, the fluorescence properties of combination of amino-modified blue CDs (CDs-3) and red CDs (CDs-4) with BA were investigated (Fig. S14a-d). The emission peaks of CDs-3/BA composites are located at 526 and 561 nm, and a significant red shift occurs for CDs-3 with emission peak at 414 nm. The optimal fluorescence emission of CDs-4/BA composites (663 and 696 nm) is also red-shifted relative to that of CDs-4 (622 nm). The afterglow emissions of CDs-1/BA and CDs-2/BA composites are located in the yellow-green region, and CDs-3/BA and CDs-4/BA composites emit red afterglow luminescence (Fig. S15a-d). Therefore, the combination of oxygen-containing CDs and nitrogen-containing CDs with BA exhibits blue-shifted and red-shifted luminescence, respectively, and the CDs combined with BA can emit afterglow luminescence.

The surface composition and structure of oxygen-containing CDs (CDs-1 and CDs-2) and oxygen-containing CDs combined with BA were investigated by FTIR and XPS spectra. As shown in the FTIR spectra (Fig. S16), CDs-1 and CDs-2 possess O-H, C-H, C=O and C-O bonds, and CDs-1/BA and CDs-2/BA composites contain B-O and B-C in addition to similar surface groups. The composition and content of surface groups in CDs-1/BA and CDs-2/BA composites are shown in Fig. S17a-d, and Table S10-13. Both CDs-1/BA and CDs-2/BA composites have C=C/C-C, C-O, C=O, B-C and B-O bonds. The contents of B-O bonds (31.13 %, 40.77 % and 42.10 % for CDs-1/BA, CDs-2/BA, and O-CDs/BA composites) gradually increases, and the degree of blue shift gradually increases for CDs-1/BA, CDs-2/BA, and O-CDs/BA composites (Table S9 and Table S13). In addition, the surface composition of CDs-3/BA and CDs-4/BA composites was explored, as shown in Fig. S18a-f and Table S14-18. The CDs-3/BA and CDs-4/BA composites have a series of characteristic peaks of O-H/N-H, C-H, C=O, B-O, C-N, C-O, B-C, and B-N bonds (Fig. S18a). The XPS spectra show that CDs-3/BA and CDs-4/BA composites contain C=C/C-C, C-N, C-O, C=O, B-N, B-C and B-O bonds, respectively. The contents of B-N bonds (27.81 %, 32.02 % and 34.33 % for CDs-4/BA, CDs-3/BA, and N-CDs/BA composites) are gradually increased, and the degree of red shift gradually increases for CDs-4/BA, CDs-3/BA, and N-CDs/BA composites (Table S9 and Table S18). The above results confirm that the B-O and B-N bonds lead to the blue and red shift of the fluorescence for oxygen-containing groups modified CDs and nitrogen-containing groups modified CDs after combining with BA, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescence mechanism of O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the characterization results from FTIR, XPS, HRMS and NMR, as well as previous studies, structural models of O-CDs, O-CDs/BA composites, N-CDs and N-CDs/BA composites were systematically constructed in Fig. 4 a-d and Fig. S12a-d [50–52]. The electronic density of states (DOS) of O-CDs, O-CDs/BA composites, N-CDs and N-CDs/BA composites are calculated, which reveals the effects of BA matrix on the surface structure and electronic properties of CDs. The total density of states (TDOS) and partial density of states (PDOS) for O-CDs, O-CDs/BA composites, N-CDs, N-CDs/BA composites, and original CDs are explored. As shown in Fig. 4e-h, the energy gaps of O-CDs and O-CDs/BA composites are 1.73 eV and 1.94 eV, respectively [53]. Compared with O-CDs, the energy gap of O-CDs/BA composites is enlarged, which is consistent with the experimental results of blue shifted fluorescence. The energy gap of N-CDs/BA composites (2.09 eV) is narrowed relative to that of N-CDs (2.22 eV), which is consistent with the red shift of fluorescence [54]. The expansion and narrowing of the energy gap of CDs are related to the formation of B-O and B-N bonds, respectively [55]. Surface modification mainly affects the LUMO levels for O-CDs, O-CDs/BA composites, N- CDs, and N-CDs/BA composites in electrochemical synthesis of CDs (Fig. S19a-c, Fig. S20 a-d, Fig. S21 a-d, and Fig. S22). The effect of B-O and B-N groups on the energy gap of CDs composites was further investigated. The calculation results indicate that the bond length of B-O and B-N bonds is 1.39 and 1.43 Å, respectively. It is found that the bond covalency decreases with the increase of bond length [56]. For chemical bond with high covalency, the bond interaction is more intense, which results in a larger luminescence energy gap [57]. The shorter B-O bonds are more covalent, which leads to the increase of LUMO energy level, thus increasing the energy gap of O-CDs/BA composites [58]. The longer B-N bonds have lower covalency, which results in a decrease in LUMO, thereby narrowing the energy gap of the N-CDs/BA composites [59–61]. The XPS spectra of B 1 s for O-CDs/BA composites revealed the presence of B-C bonds (192.38 eV) and B-O bonds (193.26 eV), as shown in Fig. 3f. The XPS spectra of B 1 s for N-CDs/BA composites display B-N (192.22 eV), B-C (193.17 eV) and B-O bonds (194.22 eV). The higher binding energy of the B-O bonds compared to the B-N bonds indicates that the B-O bonds exhibit stronger covalent characteristics [62,63]. The calculated and experimental results confirm that the B-O and B-N bonds lead to the blue-shift and red-shift of the fluorescence emission for O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites, respectively.

The intrinsic influencing factors of RTP emission for O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites were investigated. As shown in Fig. S23a-f, when O-CDs and N-CDs were physically mixed with BA at room temperature, no fluorescence and RTP emission can be observed under 365 nm UV lamp on and off. The FTIR spectra (Fig. S24) show that the physically mixed O-CDs/BA composites only contain O-H, C-O, B-O, C=O bonds, and the B-O bonds are originated from BA. Compared with the heat-treated O-CDs/BA composites, the physically mixed O-CDs/BA composites do not contain B-C bonds. The physically mixed N-CDs/BA composites contain N-H/O-H, C-O, B-O, and C=O bonds, but they do not contain B-C and B-N bonds compared to the heat-treated N-CDs/BA composites. The XPS spectra (Fig. S25a-d) confirm that the physically mixed O-CDs/BA composites do not contain B-C bonds, which is consistent with the FTIR results. In addition, the physically mixed N-CDs/BA composites do not contain B-C and B-N bonds compared with the heat-treated N-CDs/BA composites, as shown in Fig. S26 and Fig. S27a-d. It indicates that the RTP emission is due to the covalent bonds formed by CDs and BA, which stabilizes the triplet states and inhi-bits the non-radiative recombination of the triplet states.

Covalent bonds (such as B-C, B-O, and B-N) are formed between the BA matrix and the surface groups of CDs, creating a rigid network structure. This network restricts the vibrational rotation of the molecular groups on the CDs surface, suppresses non-radiative relaxation, and stabilizes the triplet excited states of the CDs [14,43,64,65]. However, the afterglow emission region of N-CDs/BA is situated in the deep red region compared to that of O-CDs/BA. The concurrent presence of singlet and triplet states results in a more complex energy level structure with a narrower energy gap [16,40]. Consequently, the rigid structure's protective effect on triplet excitons is diminished, leading to a shorter residence time for triplet excitons, and a reduced afterglow lifetime for N-CDs/BA.

A reasonable mechanism is proposed to explain the solid-state fluorescence and afterglow for O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites (Fig. 4i-l). Under the irradiation of the excitation light, the ground state electrons (S0) of O-CDs and N-CDs are jumped to the excited state (S1). Subsequently, a part of the excited electrons emit greenish-yellow fluorescence to the lowest excited state through radiative recombination, and the other part of the excited electrons transfer to the lowest excited state through non-radiative vibrational relaxation. After compounding with the BA matrix, the surface luminescent centers of O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites are bonded with B-O bonds and B-N bonds, which lead to the increase and decrease of the band gap, respectively, thus realizing the blue emission and deep red fluorescence. Furthermore, the covalent bonds such as B-C, B-O and B-N in O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites are stable and protect the triplet states from the influence of the external environment, thereby achieving RTP emission. The singlet–triplet energy gaps (ΔEST) of O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites are calculated to be 0.440 eV and 0.197 eV, respectively (Fig. S28a-b) [66].

Nowadays, counterfeit products and data leakage pose a major threat to consumers, businesses and the economy [67]. Anti-counterfeiting of fluorescent patterns and data encryption are essential to protect consumers, businesses and governments from counterfeit products and data leakage [68,69]. The excellent solid-state fluorescence and RTP luminescence properties of O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites are expected to have great potential in the field of anti-counterfeiting and encryption. Under 365 nm light excitation, the visible afterglow times for O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites are 6.0 s and 1.0 s, respectively (Fig. S29a-b and Fig. S30). As shown in Fig. 5 a-b, multi-level pattern anti-counterfeiting modes are constructed by using the fluorescence and RTP dual emission properties of O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites. Snowflake and dreamcatcher patterns were prepared with O-CDs/BA composites. The color of O-CDs/BA composites is white under sunlight. Blue and green colors are presented when irradiated and removed ultraviolet light (365 nm), respectively. The lotus flower and dragonfly patterns are made of N-CDs/BA composites, which are gray-green and deep red under sunlight and ultraviolet light (365 nm), respectively. After the ultraviolet light is removed, the pattern color is black, owing to the deep red RTP is difficult to observe. The O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites change the color by switching the fluorescence and RTP emission modes to achieve multi-level anti-counterfeiting. Furthermore, a demonstration of multilevel information encryption was achieved by leveraging the distinct characteristics of N-CDs/BA and O-CDs/BA composites in terms of their luminescent color and afterglow lifetime (Fig. 5c) [13,70]. N-CDs/BA and O-CDs/BA composites were utilized to fabricate two flower-like architectures. Under 365 nm UV illumination, the patterns of red flowers (left) and blue flowers (right) are distinctly visible. Upon turning off the UV light for a few seconds, the luminescence intensity of the red flowers rapidly diminishes and ultimately fades completely. In contrast, the blue flower structure displays distinct photoluminescent behavior, gradually shifting to green before eventually fading entirely. In addition, O-CDs/BA composites can be used for data encryption due to its excellent green RTP emission. As shown in Fig. 5d-e, numbers ('1429′, '2605′, '4783′, and '5036′) and letters ('TURE' and 'WISH') were prepared using O-CDs/BA composites and blue fluorescent materials (vitamin b1). When the UV lamp is turned on, the numbers and letters are false information. While the UV lamp is turned off, the green numbers ('149′, '20′, '478′ and '50′) and letters ('URE' and 'WIS') are real information. The above research indicates that O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites have great potential applications in the field of advanced anti-counterfeiting and multi-level data encryption.

In summary, greenish-yellow fluorescent oxygen-containing groups modified CDs (O-CDs) and nitrogen-containing groups modified CDs (N-CDs) were prepared by electrochemical method. The O-CDs and N-CDs were successfully embedded in BA matrix to form O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites with dual emission of solid-state fluorescence and RTP. The fluorescence emission of O-CDs and N-CDs is located at 570 nm and 562 nm, respectively. The fluorescence emission of O-CDs/BA composites is blue shifted to 460 nm, and the fluorescence emission of N-CDs/BA composites is red shifted to 652 and 693 nm. The RTP emission of O-CDs/BA composites is located at the 550 nm, and the RTP emission of N-CDs/BA composites is located at 666 nm and 697 nm. The experiment and calculation results disclose that the blue and red shifts of the solid-state luminescence of O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites are related to the formation of B-O and B-N bonds, respectively. With the increased contents of B-O or B-N bonds, the blue shift or red shift of the solid-state luminescence of O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites increases. The RTP emission of O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites is induced by the stable triplet states of B-C, B-O and B-N bonds formed by the combination of O-CDs/N-CDs with BA matrix. Based on the excellent solid-state fluorescence and RTP properties, O-CDs/BA and N-CDs/BA composites are applied to pattern anti-counterfeiting and data encryption, thus demonstrating potential applications in the field of anti-counterfeiting and information security. In this work, we provide a new strategy to control the luminescence energy gap of the CDs-based composites by regulating the chemical bond covalency, which realizes the color tunable fluorescence and RTP dual emission from blue to deep red region.
